sentences.text: Yes.
I waited in the bushes on the Russian side of the river, and I watched the Russian patrols-- usually two Russian soldiers-- on-- on the same side of the river.
And I watched the German patrols-- usually two Germans-- with a dog on the other side of the river.
But I-- it is not only I.
The other two people were as smart or maybe even smarter than I was at that time or more experienced in these things.
We decided that the time will be when they will change guards.
And when they change guards, somewhere there must have been a place where they were to walk into in order change guards.
And then there was, like, 10 or 15 minutes' time that nobody was working.
And that we found such a time, and we eventually came across.
We waded through the river.
The river was about up to chest-high water-- waded through the river.
At that time, I have already left the suitcases with my brothers, so they had now winter clothing.
I came back-- I-- as we crossed was late at night, and we wound up in a tavern on the German side-- German side of the occupied Poland, and we were starving.
We had-- did have some money, and we went into the tavern.
And we were very strange with that part of Poland-- we were strange in that part of Poland, and we did not know what is being served there.
There was no menu.
But we watched other people ordering.
So, people were ordering 10 or 20 scrambled eggs.
So we says, we are very hungry.
We should-- we will order 20 scrambled eggs.
So each one of us ordered 20 scrambled eggs.
And the bread we was cut long, like a roll, and we finished it all.
Eventually, I-- I did manage to get back to Krakow, when I wound up with my mother and my sister, again hoping that they would change their mind and be able to go back.
But what I did not realize that now the border is sealed to the point that I could not go back.
And this how I got stuck in the German part occupied of Poland.
I was talking to people who crossed the border coming into the German-- in the German side, and they found-- they told me about the difficulties which now is-- one is to encounter.
And I pretty much got cold feet.
I got-- I got--.
Since my mother and two sisters wouldn't want to go, I had a dilemma-- to stay with them, to go by my-- back by myself, and I decided to stay.
No.
No.
They still-- we still lived in our own apartment where we used-- where we lived before the war.

And from time to time, the Germans surrounded a certain section of Jewish population and ransacked the apartment, took away whatever they told is of value, issued edicts that people have to give-- give away gold and et cetera, et cetera-- fur coats, whatever-- anything and everything of value.
But there was no ghetto per se.
There was kind of a ghetto or self-ordained by the Jews, who lived in Krakow, only in one section.
It was Kazimierz.
We lived just outside that point-- that-- that part.
So when they surrounded the Kazimierz and for three or four days, and they conducted searches of all Jewish apartments and homes, we were actually excluded.
We were lucky that we did not have to go through that.
And this is-- this would lasted till approximately March, 1941.
In March, 1941, there was an edict issued that all Jews will have to move to a ghetto which is across the river from Krakow in a little town named Podgorze.
I'm not going to spell it because it difficult.

And they-- they formed-- they-- they formed a ghetto there and so-- moved out all Polish people from a large section of that-- part-- of that part of Krakow town, surrounded it by walls, and moved the Jews in, and also moved people into that ghetto which were not from Krakow, which were from small towns surrounding Krakow.
But since I had contacts through sports clubs and other places with people who eventually-- Jewish people who wound up to be all Polish-- Jewish policemen.
They call them [? firemen, ?] [? alderman, ?] and things like that, and all who worked in an office which was distributing jobs in German facilities.
I managed to organize jobs which were acceptable to the Germans and therefore made you, for the time being, at least, rather safe from evacuation from ghetto.
Hm? ?]
We rented wagon with two wheels, which I was the horse of, and pulled it into the ghetto.
It was quite a distance from where we lived.
And we took only the absolute necessary thing, because the place which we were assigned in ghetto was very small.
We were fortunate.
We were assigned a room and a kitchen without having another family living with us, because in many cases, an apartment like that-- a room and a kitchen-- was occupied by two or three families.
But as I said before, because we had contacts with people who had the right to make the assignments, we were given an-- an advantageous situation.
And we moved things like mattresses, sheets, pillowcases, some clothing-- as little-- as much as we could carry on that little wagon because we were allowed only one trip.
Everything else was left behind.
Beg your pardon?
No, I don't.
It is so long back I wouldn't really be able to recall the thoughts.
The thoughts were more about the future rather than the past.
We have already learned by then that things of value have not-- no great meaning except as they serve you for the moment.
You only need what you need now.
You don't need what you will need a year from  now because you don't know what will happen a year from now.
Therefore, all these things we left behind had little meaning to us.
Of course, we left most-- most-- all of our furniture, all of the stuff which you accumulate over many years in a-- in a family life.
We took with us only what we needed-- what we knew we will need for next day, next week, or next month.
I was fortunate to get an assignment to a Polish furrier who was given [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] fur skins from the Germans to-- to make fur coats for the wives of the German officers.
And during their furloughs, they used to take him home with them to Germany.
And for that purpose, I was-- I received a pass to be able to get out of the ghetto every morning and come back every evening without any supervision.
I just had to wear an-- an armband with the Star of David while I was traveling to and from.
The travel consisted of walking because by that time, Jews were not allowed to use trolley cars anymore.
Sometimes before the ghetto, Jews were allowed to use trolley cars but had to stand in the very back.
But this was eventually changed, and at that point, Jews were not allowed to drive-- to ride wherever-- to ride trolley cars at all, so I had to walk.
Well, there was no real pay in money.
Nevertheless, I did get some pocket money from the owner of that place because I did very well for him.
He was making money out of me being there.
And he also was giving me different things, foodstuffs, to bring into ghetto.
At that time, I was wearing boots which were about, like, three-quarter high and were very loose at the top.
And I always had stuffed in there some stuff, and I walked into a-- into ghetto with them in my boots.
And this how we survived, actually, above and beyond the-- the ration cards, which were a starving diet.
My mother was not working.
My sister was working.
She-- my sister eventually became a dressmaker.
She after-- she had to interrupt her high school, and she went to a Jewish-run vocational school where she learned dressmaking.
And she became very good at it.
She was making beautiful wedding gowns and very elegant gowns.
And she got an assignment because of her talent to do these things.
And she was safe for a while with the documents which they have issued to her, just like I was safe for a while with the documents which I was issued by the SS, having worked with that furrier.
I don't know.
Who was wearing them?
Oh, they were for Germans.
It was all for Germans.
It was outside the ghetto.
It was for Germans.
Who were the people who run that shop?
I really don't know.

On June 6, 1942, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the Germans assembled all the Jews in a plaza, and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] each one of us had to-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] had to stand in front of a German officer who-- who inspected your identification card and inspected your papers of your work assignment.
And they decided whether the work assignment is important for the Germans.
Therefore, you would be allowed to stay in ghetto and continue or that it's not important with Germans.
Therefore, you will be moved to, as they say, [GERMAN] to another location.
My sister had the-- passed and received a stamp, a special stamp on her identification card indicating that she's allowed to stay in ghetto.
So was I.
I got also such a stamp.
My mother did not since she had no assignment.
And on the 6th of June, my mother and many hundreds or a couple-- two or three thousand of people-- were taken out of ghetto.
And to best of my knowledge, the whole transport wound up in Belzec, where they were all exterminated.

[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] After June segregation, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I realized that my assignment to the furrier is not going to be good enough next time such a thing will happen, and therefore, I had to change my assignment.
But I have to backtrack here because a few months before that, I was called by the Jewish police to arrive in their headquarters and was told that my younger brother is outside the ghetto.
And he was brought back from the Russian part of-- of occupied Poland.
And the person who smuggled him through wants to be paid off, or he will simply tell the Germans that he is a Jew, and he will be done.
I had-- he wanted 600 zloty at that time.
And since I still had the pass to go out of the ghetto, I could go out and see him, but I could not bring him in because he had no identifications.
I had my father's gold watch, which was one of the treasures which we have still kept.

I had to sell it up on the black market to get the 600 zloty-- well, I got more than that-- and go out again and pay to the man the 600 zloty and had to come back to ghetto and go to the office which assigned jobs and-- and issued identification cards, which-- where I had some friends, because this was almost impossible to get at that point.
But I was fortunate that my friends came through, and they made me a set of identification card and an assignment to-- for my brother to work on the Krakow airport.
And this how I managed to bring my brother back into the ghetto.
Everybody in the ghetto had to have identification card and job assignment.
Otherwise, he was lost.
So actually, my brother started working on the airport before I did.
I was still working at the furriers.
But after June 6, when I realized that this assignment is not going to save me, I had to change my assignment.
I went back to the friend-- my friends in the office.
There was an [GERMAN] and I managed to get a change of assignment, also to the airport.
So my younger brother and I were working at the airport, my sister was working for the dressmakers for the SS wives, and my mother was gone.
My older brother was left behind in Russia.
Now, my-- my younger brother, when he came back, was smuggled in-- into the ghetto.
Told-- ?] I asked him, Manek, why did you come?
Where is Milek?
He said, Milek is dead.
The Germans occupied Kiev-- well, after they actually-- after the liquidation of that farm in eastern Poland, the Russians moved all these people further east, and he wound up in Kiev.
I know very little, if anything, about what he did in Kiev, where he lived in Kiev.
All I know from our younger brother that when the Germans arrived, they assembled all the Jews and killed them, and my older brother was killed.
And this is why my younger brother decided that he is not going to stay, he is going to go back home-- what he thought is home.
He did not know enough about ghetto to know that this is not home anymore, that he probably would have been better off trying to stay in Russia, maybe even going further east.
He probably would have been-- would have been alive today.

In August, 1942, the Germans again assembled everybody in the-- in the ghetto and provided an additional stamp on the identification card for people who were allowed to stay and again had another transport.
This was only three months later.
But at that time, I already worked on the airport, my brother worked on the airport, and my sister worked for the SS.
And we all three were allowed to stay.
When we arrived the following morning at the airport, my-- my younger brother and I, we were assembled on the plaza, and the German colonel who was in charge of the airport had a speech to us.
And he said that he doesn't-- he needs us very badly.
There were about 200 of us, about 100 men and 100 women.

He needs us because we're doing good work, and he doesn't want to go through the trouble again of the Germans-- of the SS deciding who is going to stay and who is going to be evacuated or taken away to extermination camp.
He's going to provide a camp of his own, right on the airport.
And we have not gone back anymore.
He managed to get through the air force, through the Luftwaffe, permission to form his own what he called a labor camp, but was actually adjunct to the Krakow ghetto.
And he built two barracks, or there were [INAUDIBLE] two barracks built already-- in which the men moved into one, women moved into the other.
He surrounded them by a wire to make sure the SS would approve of the kind of arrangement that he made.
He put a wire fence between the men and women's barracks, and hired-- [?
I mean ?] [? hired-- ?] ordered some Luftwaffe or air-- air force personnel to have-- to keep guard overnight in front of the entrance.
I don't remember [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] whether we-- whether we went back to ghetto or whether we-- this was it, we were stuck there.
No.
This is the most difficult thing.
Things were changing so rapidly, and thoughts were changing so rapidly.
The situation was-- was changing so rapidly that your thinking was not-- never at the past.
You was-- always you were thinking of what is ahead.
There was one situation worthwhile explaining while I was working at the airport and still living in the ghetto.
While I was working at the airport, I was assigned to a shop which was repairing fur coats for the German flyers.
Some of these fur coats were torn beyond repair, and I was allowed to cut into pieces and use them to repair others, as well as have some material left.
The German officers at the airport, the Luftwaffe officers, very quickly realized that I can do something for them, and I did.
I learned how to make slippers out of fur, and gloves-- and also gloves.
And I made slippers and gloves.
They weren't gloves with five fingers, was like-- how would you call those which have one--
Mittens.
That's right, like mittens.
And they were coming every day.
I want two pairs of mittens.
I'm going on a-- on a furlough, and I want to have mittens for my wife or my daughter, whatever.
So I was-- I was busy all the time making mittens that I-- I wasn't fixing furs anymore.
They weren't interested in the furs.
The Krakow ghetto was actually a stopover airport for many wounded Germans who came from the east coast-- from the east-- [INAUDIBLE] it's not east coast-- I'm sorry-- the east front.
But these Germans, the officers, German officers, who were assigned to the Krakow-- they didn't care about them.
They only cared about what can they do for themselves.
This was while we still were living in ghetto.
One day a train arrived on the siding inside the airport, and several people were taken away from their work, from their job, to unload the train.
The train consisted of equipment-- primarily uniforms, boots, socks, belts-- what-- shirts, et cetera-- for many thousands of-- of soldiers.
We were designated to carry that to different warehouses.
They had several warehouses.
Shirts went here, boots were somewhere else, et cetera.
The following morning, we were brought by truck from ghetto, and we went, as usual, back to work.
After a few minutes at work, a soldier came up and he says, Joseph, you are to report to the [GERMAN] commandant-- whatever-- the man who's in charge of the uniforms and equipment.
And I did-- I come there, and there were several people already sitting.
Some of them were Jews and some of them were Polish, because Polish people also worked at the airport, coming in every morning from the outside to work.
And there was a long table set up, and behind the table there were German officers with a marker [GERMAN].
It's something similar to American MP, military police.
And the commandant of the group which we were-- we were responsible to was sitting at a desk at a distant corner.
He was just the-- more like an overseer, but not involved in the interrogation which was to take place.
And each one of us was called to stand up.
A cardboard was put under the-- our shoes.
They penciled our shoes around the cardboard and walked away with every one of them, and marked this one's Joseph's, this one is [INAUDIBLE].
And they walked behind the counter, behind the door, and disappeared.
After a few minutes they came back, and bringing my card, he says that my marks match the marks that he found in a-- in-- in a-- in a storage room.
What happened there, somebody broke in overnight and stole many hundreds of boots.
And my-- now they said that my shoes matched the marks on the window through which, supposedly, the robber-- the burglars came in.
At that time I spoke fluent German, and the German officer who interrogated me says, well, your things match.
You were here, you stole the shoes.
And-- and he says, we interviewed all these people, and he points to the three Pollacks there and says, you did it.
So, what do you have to say for yourself?
I-- I-- I stood up and in, as I said, fluent German, I told them I could not have been here overnight.
These people are testifying against me because I-- I am a Jew, and they're trying to save their own skin.
Probably they did it.
Yes, but your shoes match.
I said, anybody's shoes would match.
So he says, why would they be accusing you?
Who are you to them?
I said, I am a Jew, this is enough.
He stood up-- he was seven feet tall.
He says in German to me, I don't believe in what SS does to the Jews.
I am here to solve a crime, and if you telling me the truth, you will be free.
And he looks at a distant [INAUDIBLE].
And I explained to him that we are being taken out of ghetto every morning, put on a truck, counted, coming-- driven to the airport.
Before the ramp is open to let us in, we are counted.
When we are assembled to go back to the ghetto, we are counted.
When we arrive at the ghetto again, before we are allowed to get in, we are counted.
I said, so of course, I could not have been here overnight.
And he looked to the corner, to the officer who's in charge of our department at the time, and I saw him shaking his head that this how it is, that I couldn't have been here.
So this is when he stood up and he said, you are free to go.
I don't play the SS game.
Something in-- to this extent.
So this was, in my opinion, a significant thing in my life which probably saved my life.
Because the three Polish people who worked there, never anybody heard about them anymore.
They must have been killed-- arrested, and eventually disposed of.
As I said, this was before we actually were living already on the airport.
While living at the airport, I still continued working where I did, and things were reasonably good there.
We were given a reasonable amount of food, and we did good-- good work.
We had a-- a Jewish doctor in the camp, and something happened-- the-- the Jewish doctor was ordered to give us some kind of injections which were given into your thigh.
And I was given that injection-- everybody did-- and it must have been an unclean needle or something that.
My-- my leg swelled terribly.
I had to be, eventually, operated to-- to remove the pus from my thigh.
And the colonel wanted me to have the operation in the-- at the airport hospital, but the German doctors refused to operate on a Jew.
Hold that thought.
We're going to go to the next tape.
I don't want to run out of tape.
